← the Atlas

Remote terminals over SSH

Features· budding ·accepted ·JUN 21, 2026

The skeleton plan for kolu#951 — remote terminals over SSH, as one R1–R9 phase tree. R1–R4 shipped (foundation · local kaval survivor · the remote-ssh spike · the standalone arivu awareness daemon), and R7 shipped early (mirrorRemoteSurface is now a total dual, procedures forwarded); the remaining work R5·R6·R8·R9 is a linear roadmap defined in this note. Build logs live in the child notes — pty-daemon (R2), kaval-sessions (R3), arivu (R4).

Contents

  • User-facing description
  • Architecture-level changes
  • Implementation details
    • R5 — overflow-recovery loop
    • R6 — @kolu/terminal-workspace: one library, one surface, two homes
    • R7 — mirrorRemoteSurface is now a total dual — shipped
    • R8 — kolu dials remotes + the fold
    • R9 — canvas multiplex + validate
  • History

The skeleton plan for #951 — remote terminals over SSH. Every host — this machine and each ssh remote — runs the same kaval daemon; kolu-server keys its terminal endpoint by hostId, and you switch hosts from the ChromeBar, kinda like tmux sessions. R1–R4 are shipped — the foundation, the local kaval survivor, the remote-ssh spike, and the standalone arivu awareness daemon — and R7 shipped early (the surface mirror is now a total dual, procedures included; it stood alone, needing neither R6 nor kolu). What remains (R5 · R6 · R8 · R9) is one linear roadmap, defined in this note: harden reconnect, grow @kolu/terminal-workspace (one surface — awareness + fs/git — two homes), dial remotes and wire those into the tile, then multiplex the canvas. The shipped branches’ build logs live in the child notes: pty-daemon (R2), kaval-sessions (R3), arivu (R4).

User-facing description

kolu
hostslocalnix@prodstaging+ host⟳ live
local
kolu · master
◜codexworking
nix@prodssh
kolu · feat/dial-ssh #1412 ✓
●claudeawaiting you
nix@prodssh
infra · deploy
◜—working
local
notes · main
○claudeidle

You switch hosts from the ChromeBar — kinda like tmux sessions — and a remote host’s terminals sit on the canvas beside your local ones. A remote tile wears an ssh badge and carries the same live awareness a local tile does — git branch/dirty, PR status, agent state, and a green dot when it’s moving bytes — and it survives a network blip or a kolu-server deploy with full scrollback. One local host is always present; remotes are added on demand, auto-detected from your ssh config.

Architecture-level changes

One endpoint, bound to a dialed kaval. A kaval is a daemon you dial — local over a unix socket, remote over ssh — so remote is not a second backend. There is one TerminalEndpoint, bound to a HostLocation-resolved kaval endpoint ({kind:"local"} today, {kind:"remote",host} in R8). That resolver is the sole place a tile maps to its kaval; everything downstream talks to the backend and never asks “which kind?”. No RemoteTerminalEndpoint — R8 adds the ssh driver as an additive sibling of R2’s local driver, never a retrofit.

Two volatility axes stay open (R1/R2 closed the rest):

Axis — changes for its own reasonEncapsulated by
Where a terminal’s state lives — this machine vs an ssh hostthe HostLocation-resolved kaval endpoint — one impl + a discriminator, not a second backend
How the backend reaches its agent — transport · framing · reconnectHostSession + @kolu/surface/links/stdio — unix socket today, ssh in R8

Zero domain knowledge crosses the transport (Hickey’s cut): the agent runs the unmodified sensors and the boundary ships their values. An earlier per-domain design (RemoteGitInfoProvider, …) was transport adapters wearing domain costumes; the single-endpoint seam dissolved them.

The awareness fold is domain logic, not electricity

R8’s fold merges each remote AwarenessValue into the co-owned terminalMetadata entry and splits it across the persisted/live fence — pr·agent·foreground through the quiet live mutator, cwd·git·lastAgentCommand·lastActivityAt through the one that arms autosave. Lose the split and you either re-arm the agent-firehose (a perf regression) or stop persisting. It speaks the whole terminal domain and the autosave fence, so it stays inline in kolu-server — never a @kolu/* package.

Already paid for, so R5–R9 are narrow. @kolu/surface-nix-host ships HostSession, provisionAgent (ships the derivation — a darwin parent drives a linux remote with no cross-builder), and resolveSystem; @kolu/surface ships mirrorRemoteSurface (the per-primitive mirror). R2 already shipped the host-count-agnostic spine: @kolu/surface-daemon-supervisor and the per-connect system.version handshake. So R8 is a driver + fold behind shipped seams; R7 completed the surface mirror ( #1505 ); R6 grows arivu’s fs/git; R5 is one wire signal.

Implementation details

The whole epic as one tree — R1–R4 shipped, R5–R9 remain. Each underlined row jumps to where that phase is defined: the shipped branches to their child-note build logs, and the five remaining phases to their own ### section just below on this page.

REMOTE TERMINALS OVER SSH · #951
✓R1 · Foundation — seam · framework · engine#981·984·1004
✓R2 · kaval — local PTY survival→ pty-daemon
✓R2.1–R2.5 · inversion → door → survival → inventory#1292…1458
✓R3 · the remote-ssh spike→ kaval-sessions
✓R3.1–R3.6 · kaval-tui dials local + ssh#1364…1378
✓R4 · arivu — the awareness daemon→ arivu
✓R4.1–R4.5 · sensors → daemon → --host → fleet#1413…1497
○R5 · Overflow-recovery loopnext · defined ↓
○R6 · terminal-workspace — one surface (awareness + fs/git)defined ↓
✓R7 · mirrorRemoteSurface → total dual (procedures forwarded)#1505
○R8 · kolu dials remotes + the foldneeds R6·R7 ↓
○R9 · canvas multiplex + validatedefined ↓

The shipped branches R1–R4 are detailed in their child notes (and the PRs above); R7 shipped early (it stood alone — neither R6 nor kolu). What follows is the roadmap of remaining work — each defined here. R5 · R6 are independent — do them in any order; R8 needs R6 + R7 (✓); R9 needs R8.

R5 — overflow-recovery loop

~130 LoC w/ tests; the next step. kaval sheds a slow attach subscriber by ending its iterator, today indistinguishable on the wire from a PTY exit, so the client treats the drop as terminal and freezes scrollback (onOverflow exists but is unwired). Thread a typed overflow → re-attach signal through the attach contract so the client re-attaches for a fresh snapshot (reusing the existing onRetry reset), distinct from PTY-exit. Ships independently of the remote work: a slow subscriber is reproducible on the local socket, so it is CI-testable today, and the fix hardens the attach contract for both transports — a clean prerequisite for the remote reconnect R8 leans on. herdr vs kolu flagged the same gap.

R6 — @kolu/terminal-workspace: one library, one surface, two homes

The host-side library that knows a terminal’s working directory becomes @kolu/terminal-workspace — the shipped @kolu/terminal-awareness (R4.1) grown to cover fs/git too, run in-process locally (kolu) and hosted by arivu remotely: the same two homes the sensors already proved. It serves one @kolu/surface surface; the primitives differ only in kind:

  • awareness (collection) + activity (stream) — the derived status badge + the green-dot liveness. Shipped.
  • fsListAll · fsReadFile · gitStatus · gitDiff (procedures) + subscribeRepoChange / FileChange (watcher streams) — the Code tab’s raw reads + live deltas. New in R6, lifted from kolu-server’s localEndpoint (a thin wrapper over kolu-git + serve-dir) into the shared library.

This kills the duplication wart: without it, arivu would reimplement fs/git and kolu would carry two fs/git impls (its own local, arivu’s remote). Instead there is one — in-process locally, via arivu remotely. Only the thin fs/git wrapper moves; the terminal-endpoint orchestration around it (spawn · adopt · the registry) stays kolu’s and gets a remote impl in R8. (One surface; R8 mirrors it whole — via R7’s total mirror — folding the awareness collection into terminalMetadata and forwarding the fs/git procedures. Nothing is folded into AwarenessValue — procedures were never part of it.)

R7 — mirrorRemoteSurface is now a total dual — shipped #1505

mirrorRemoteSurface is the consume-side dual of implementSurface — and a dual must mirror every primitive its producer wires. implementSurface wires procedures too, but the mirror’s SurfaceSink carried only cells/collections/streams/events: a leaky half-mirror, harmless only because every surface mirrored so far had zero procedures. R7 closed the gap by kind: streaming state stays PUSH (subscribed and folded into the sink); a procedure is PULL (a local call runs on the remote and returns), so it can’t sit in the push-only sink — it comes back as a forwarding stub. The return is therefore { procedures, done }: procedures.<ns>.<verb>(input) relays to the remote (total — every procedure gets a stub, since a passthrough has no standing cost), and done is the former settle-on-link-close promise. Graft the stubs into a re-serve and serve ∘ mirror ≈ identity — the location-transparency the whole epic rests on — pinned in-repo by a hermetic unit test (implementSurface → directLink → mirror → re-serve → call). A @kolu/surface change → the drishti merge-gate.

Proven by drishti — the canonical external second consumer. drishti is the browser ↔ Bun ↔ ssh process-monitor that already mirrors per-host process surfaces (the graduation that first proved @kolu/surface is electricity), and it was read-only. R7’s external proof is drishti gaining a “Kill process” action — the first forwarded procedure on a mirrored surface, exercised end-to-end (browser → Bun → ssh → remote agent → kill(pid)), zero kolu, riding the same drishti gate every @kolu/surface change already passes (companion PR). Because that proof lives entirely in drishti’s own surface, R7 stood alone — it needed neither R6 nor kolu; R8 now consumes a proven total mirror for arivu’s fs/git, not a speculative one.

R8 — kolu dials remotes + the fold

The linchpin — it needs R6 (the shared fs/git library) and R7 (the total mirror). kolu-server gains the N-endpoint registry + the ssh driver — the {kind:"remote",host} arm, the (host,id) join, a persisted location — reusing the same getHostSession the spike (R3) built. It dials the host’s kaval for the PTY (via the shipped frontDaemonOverStdio) → a remote tile; and dials its arivu over a long-lived HostSession, mirroring the whole terminal-workspace surface (R7) into one local handle. Because the mirror is total, kolu binds the remote TerminalEndpoint to that single handle and runs the same logic as against the in-process local surface — never asking “which kind?”:

  • the awareness collection + activity stream are subscribed into kolu’s sink, which folds each AwarenessValue into the tile’s co-owned terminalMetadata across the persisted/live fence (see the callout) and lights the green dot;
  • the fs/git procedures (fsReadFile/gitDiff + the watcher deltas) are forwarded on demand for the Code tab.

location is co-owned with terminalMetadata, persisted kolu-side, re-validated on reconnect against the remote kaval’s live cwd. (Paste/upload is a TerminalEndpoint concern — served behind the same seam with the PRECONDITION_FAILED guard — not awareness, not fs/git.) The result: a remote terminal as capable as a local one — PTY, awareness badge, green dot, working Code tab. The shape is the lead diagram above.

Adoption — a dial joins, never clobbers. If the host already runs a kaval it is adopted (the same adoptOrEnsure spine the local boot uses); a duplicate is impossible by construction (the pid-gate’s atomic link(2) EEXIST). On dial:

the remote kaval is…kolu does
absentprovision + spawn fresh (adoptOrEnsure → ensure)
live · wire-compatibleadopt — connect, never kill; its PTYs reconcile in
live · a build behindadopt + an “update pending” nudge (orthogonal)
live · contract-skewedrecycle (kill → respawn) — only on a typed DaemonContractSkewError
unreachable (bad dial)bounded retry, then degraded — never killed

One kaval per remote host: dialing joins its terminals (“the prod terminals”), never racing to kill them.

R9 — canvas multiplex + validate

The UI: an active-kaval strip (local always present), a per-tile host hint, an ssh-config host picker — kinda like tmux sessions. Then validate the feel on real use: does N-kavals-at-once beat tmux-style one-at-a-time switching? Fall back to switch if multiplex doesn’t earn its keep. The granularity call lands here, validated, not up front.

History

  • 2026-05-26 → 28 — R1 shipped: the TerminalEndpoint seam ( #981 ), the framework gaps + @kolu/surface-nix-host ( #984 ), and the shared sensor engine ( #1004 ).
  • R2 re-sequenced before R3/R5 — a whole-plan /lowy pass showed that building the multi-client survivor once collapses the later phases; R2 split into pty-daemon and shipped end-to-end (R2.1 → R2.5), closing #671 (terminals survive a deploy).
  • Multi-host end state set 2026-06-11: 1 local + N ssh kavals, host-switched in the ChromeBar; R2’s redo baked the host-count-agnostic shapes (hostId-keyed status, the HostLocation discriminator) so R3 retrofits nothing.
  • Renumbered to one R1–R9 tree (2026-06-21) — shipped branches R1–R4 (was R-1/R-1.5/R-4/R-2/R-3 + colliding child P/B schemes), and the remaining work lifted out of the child notes into a linear R5–R9 roadmap defined here (so the forward plan lives in one place; fs/git joins awareness in one library, @kolu/terminal-workspace, as its own phase R6; completing mirrorRemoteSurface to a total dual got its own phase R7 — proven via drishti’s “Kill process” action, so it stands alone, before kolu’s fold (R8) depends on it). Git holds the old labels. Cf. ghostex vs kolu, herdr vs kolu, kaval vs zmosh.
  • R7 shipped (2026-06-21, #1505 ) — mirrorRemoteSurface returns { procedures, done }: streaming primitives fold into the sink as before, every procedure comes back as a forwarding stub (procedures.<ns>.<verb>(input) → runs on the remote, returns). serve ∘ mirror ≈ identity is pinned by an in-repo unit test; the drishti companion PR adds the external “Kill process” forwarded-procedure proof. R8’s fold now consumes a proven total mirror.

Referenced by

  • arivu — terminal awareness as an ephemeral surface on kaval
  • Ghostex vs. kolu remote-terminals
  • Kaval sessions — dial daemons, multiplex the canvas
  • R2 — kaval, the standalone PTY daemon
